copy_text_samples: A safety-first, quick-reference herb guide for people whose anxiety worsened after gabapentin or HRT. Education-only; not medical advice.
What Most Herb Guides Get Wrong About Your Prescriptions
If gabapentin or HRT made your anxiety worse — not better — this is the safety-first quick-reference built for exactly that situation. No guessing. No forum rabbit holes.
Woman sitting calmly at a desk reviewing a printed herb reference guide, warm natural light, lifestyle photography
Safety shield icon for contraindication quick-scan tables
Pill and herb leaf interaction icon for medicated readers

General herb references list benefits and broad cautions — but they were written for a baseline population not managing gabapentin, HRT, blood pressure drugs, or antidepressants. If you're on a prescription, the standard guide is missing the chapter that applies to you.
Most herb guides were written for people not on any medications
Open herb reference book next to prescription medication bottles on a table, illustrating the gap between general herb guides and medicated readers

Herb-drug interaction research has accumulated in clinical literature. The gap isn't in the science — it's in whether that science has been organized into a format a non-clinician can use before making a decision, not after something goes wrong.
The interaction data exists — it just hasn't been translated for home use
Person reading a clinical research paper at home, highlighting text, warm desk lamp light, illustrating the translation gap between research and everyday use

A safety-first reference starts with your medication situation and works outward: what are the conservative starting points, what should you flag first, and what should you avoid entirely. That structure — contraindication scan before herb selection — is what's missing from most guides.
Triage before trial changes the entire decision
Printed checklist with herb names and checkboxes on a desk, representing a contraindication quick-scan triage process

Doctors unfamiliar with herbs often say 'just avoid it all' — honest but unhelpful. A structured quick-scan reference gives you something concrete to bring to that conversation, so you're not starting from zero every time.
Vague advice leaves you stuck between guessing and avoiding everything
Woman looking frustrated at a laptop screen showing conflicting forum advice about herbs and medications

When anxiety or dizziness spikes after a new medication or hormone adjustment, the cause isn't always obvious. A simple log that captures what you tried, when, and what changed gives you real data to work with — and something meaningful to share at your next appointment.
Tracking patterns after a med change is how you find what actually helps
Person writing in a simple health tracking journal next to a cup of herbal tea, representing the Patch and Pill Change Tracker

I'd been avoiding herbs entirely because I couldn't find clear interaction info anywhere. This gave me a starting point I actually trusted. I finally felt like I had a process instead of just guessing.
I brought the quick-scan sheet to my doctor's appointment. She said it was the clearest summary of herb-drug considerations she'd seen from a patient. That alone was worth it.
After gabapentin made my anxiety worse, I was scared to try anything else. The contraindication tables helped me understand what to check first. I didn't feel like I was flying blind anymore.
I expected another generic herb list. This is completely different — organized around what you're already taking, not just what the herb does in isolation. That's the reference I needed.
A safety-screened herb reference built for people already on prescriptions
The Honest Herbalist Handbook open to the Contraindication Quick-Scan Tables section, shown on a clean desk with a cup of herbal tea
What readers say about finally having a safety-first herb reference
The Honest Herbalist - Clinical Grade Botanicals

Common Interaction Tripwires Checklist: sedating herbs, alcohol, and common anxiety/sleep meds — what to double-check first
Med/HRT Conversation Pack: what to track and how to describe what you're experiencing to your clinician
